description: Recurring sale of software access via subscriptions. Levers are recurring revenue (MRR/ARR), expansion vs. churn (retention), unit economics (CAC, LTV), and funnel conversion (trial/free → paid). The defining grain trap is recurring vs. one-time money: only the subscription's periodic price is recurring revenue.

metrics.name: Monthly / Annual Recurring Revenue (MRR / ARR)
metrics.definition: Normalized recurring revenue from active subscriptions as of a point in time. ARR = MRR * 12.
metrics.formula: SUM(monthly_normalized_price) over subscriptions ACTIVE on the as-of date; annual plans contribute plan_price/12; ARR = MRR * 12.
metrics.grain: Subscription grain, evaluated as a point-in-time snapshot on a chosen date — NOT a sum of invoices/payments over a period. Normalize every plan to a monthly amount (annual → /12, quarterly → /3). Only count subscriptions whose status is active and whose start<=as_of<end on that date.
anti_patterns: SUM(invoice_amount) or SUM(payment) over a period mixes one-time charges (setup fees, overages, services) into MRR and double-counts annual prepayments — MRR is a snapshot of recurring price, not collected cash.
Adding an annual plan's full year price to MRR without dividing by 12 overstates MRR ~12x.
Summing subscription rows when an account has multiple/overlapping or upgraded subscriptions inflates MRR — dedupe to the currently-active subscription per account.
Forgetting the as-of date filter (start<=as_of<end) sums historical + cancelled subscriptions as if still active.
metrics.sane_range: currency, strictly positive; ARR is exactly 12*MRR by construction.
needs_concepts: subscriptions with start/end dates and status

metrics.name: Net Revenue Retention (NRR / NDR)
metrics.definition: Recurring revenue retained from a starting cohort over a period, INCLUDING expansion and contraction but EXCLUDING new logos acquired during the period.
metrics.formula: (starting_MRR + expansion_MRR - contraction_MRR - churned_MRR) / NULLIF(starting_MRR, 0), measured on the SAME cohort of accounts active at period start.
metrics.grain: Account-cohort grain. Fix the set of accounts active at the start of the window; track ONLY those accounts' MRR at period end. New accounts that signed up during the window must be excluded from both numerator and denominator.
anti_patterns: Including new-logo MRR in the numerator inflates NRR — that is gross new revenue, not retention of the cohort.

Double-counting expansion: if you compute end_MRR for the cohort directly, do NOT then also ADD an expansion term on top — pick one method (end-of-period cohort MRR ÷ start MRR, OR start + expansion − contraction − churn), never both.
Counting subscription rows instead of distinct accounts lets one account's plan change register as both a churn and a new sale.
Comparing period-summed revenue instead of point-in-time MRR conflates billing timing with retention.
metrics.sane_range: ratio, typically 0.8..1.4; NRR > 1 is NORMAL and GOOD (expansion outweighs churn) — do not treat >1 as a bug. Best-in-class B2B is ~1.1–1.3. Values far above ~1.5 usually mean new logos leaked into the cohort.
needs_concepts: MRR per account at two points in time

metrics.name: Gross Revenue Churn Rate
metrics.definition: Share of starting recurring revenue lost to cancellations and downgrades over a period. Gross churn EXCLUDES expansion (it cannot net out, so it is bounded 0..1).
metrics.formula: (churned_MRR + contraction_MRR) / NULLIF(starting_MRR, 0) over the cohort active at period start.
metrics.grain: Account/MRR-cohort grain. Numerator = MRR lost from accounts in the starting cohort during the window; denominator = that same cohort's starting MRR. Lost MRR is measured against each account's start-of-period price.
anti_patterns: Subtracting expansion in the numerator turns gross churn into NET churn and can go negative — gross churn must never net expansion.
Using end-of-period or whole-base MRR (including new logos) as the denominator understates churn — the denominator is the STARTING cohort's MRR.
Counting churned subscription rows rather than churned MRR mixes logo loss with dollar loss.
A churn rate above 1 or below 0 signals a wrong denominator/cohort, not real data.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1; healthy B2B monthly gross revenue churn is ~0.005–0.02 (annualized ~0.05–0.20). Never negative, never > 1.
needs_concepts: churned and downgraded MRR in the period

metrics.name: Logo / Customer Churn Rate
metrics.definition: Share of customers (accounts) active at period start who fully cancelled during the period. Counted in distinct accounts, not dollars and not subscriptions.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT churned_account_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT account_id ACTIVE at period start), 0).
metrics.grain: Distinct-account grain. Denominator = accounts active at the START of the window; numerator = the subset of THOSE accounts that fully churned (all subscriptions ended) during the window. Pre-aggregate to one row per account before counting.
anti_patterns: Counting subscription rows instead of DISTINCT accounts: a multi-seat or multi-product account looks like several churns and can push the ratio above 1.
Putting newly-acquired-then-cancelled accounts in the numerator but not the start-of-period denominator breaks the cohort and can exceed 1.
A downgrade is NOT a logo churn — logo churn is only full account cancellation; mixing in contractions over-counts.
Logo churn > 1 is ALWAYS a bug (you cannot lose more accounts than you started with) — unlike NRR, this rate has a hard ceiling of 1.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (never > 1, never negative); SMB B2C runs higher, enterprise B2B often <0.01/month.
needs_concepts: account active status at period boundaries

metrics.name: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)
metrics.definition: Sales and marketing spend divided by the number of NEW customers acquired in the same period.
metrics.formula: SUM(sales_and_marketing_spend) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT new_account_id), 0) over the same period.
metrics.grain: Account grain for the count; period grain for spend. 'New' = accounts whose first paid subscription started in the window. Spend and new-account counts must cover the SAME period (and same channel/segment if computing a non-blended CAC).
anti_patterns: Dividing spend by ALL customers (not just newly acquired) understates CAC.
Counting trials or free signups as acquisitions inflates the denominator — CAC denominator is new PAID customers.
Mismatched windows (this month's spend ÷ cumulative customers) produces a meaningless ratio.
Counting subscription rows instead of distinct new accounts double-counts multi-product buyers.
metrics.sane_range: currency, positive; only meaningful relative to LTV and payback. Self-serve B2C CAC is low; enterprise B2B can be thousands+.

metrics.name: Customer Lifetime Value (LTV) and LTV:CAC
metrics.definition: Expected gross-margin recurring revenue from a customer over their lifetime; LTV:CAC compares that value to acquisition cost.
metrics.formula: LTV = (ARPA_monthly * gross_margin_pct) / NULLIF(monthly_logo_churn_rate, 0); LTV:CAC = LTV / NULLIF(CAC, 0). (Average lifetime in months = 1 / churn_rate.)
metrics.grain: Account grain, derived from period-level rates. ARPA, gross margin, and churn must all be on a consistent periodicity (e.g. all monthly). Churn must be the logo churn RATE (a fraction), so 1/churn yields lifetime in periods.
anti_patterns: Using revenue ARPA without multiplying by gross margin overstates LTV — LTV is gross-margin value, not top-line.
Dividing by an annual churn rate while ARPA is monthly (or vice versa) mismatches periods and skews lifetime by 12x.
Churn rate of 0 makes LTV infinite — guard with NULLIF and treat as 'undefined / use a capped horizon' rather than a huge number.
Summing historical revenue per customer is realized value, not LTV; do not conflate the two.
metrics.sane_range: LTV in currency, positive. LTV:CAC ratio typically 3..5 is healthy; <1 means you lose money per customer; >>5 may mean under-investment in growth (or a churn/margin error).

metrics.name: Average Revenue Per Account / User (ARPA / ARPU)
metrics.definition: Recurring revenue divided by the number of active accounts (ARPA) or active users (ARPU) in a period.
metrics.formula: total_MRR / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT active_account_id), 0)  -- ARPU divides by distinct active users instead.
metrics.grain: Recurring-revenue snapshot ÷ distinct active entities for the SAME point in time. Use point-in-time MRR over distinct active accounts; do not average per-subscription prices.
anti_patterns: AVG(plan_price) averages plans, not accounts — a single account on several add-ons should be summed to one account, then averaged across accounts.
Dividing MRR by subscription-row count instead of distinct accounts deflates ARPA for multi-product accounts.
Mixing one-time revenue into the numerator (see MRR) inflates ARPA.
Using all-time accounts in the denominator instead of those active in the period understates ARPA.
metrics.sane_range: currency per account/user, strictly positive; B2B ARPA >> B2C ARPU as a rule.

metrics.name: CAC Payback Period
metrics.definition: Number of months of gross-margin recurring revenue from a new customer needed to recoup the cost of acquiring them.
metrics.formula: CAC / NULLIF(ARPA_monthly * gross_margin_pct, 0)  -- result is in months.
metrics.grain: Account-cohort grain, expressed in months. CAC and ARPA must reference the same customer cohort and currency; ARPA must be monthly and margin-adjusted so the quotient reads as months.
anti_patterns: Using revenue ARPA (no gross margin) understates payback — recovery is on contribution margin, not top-line revenue.
Annual ARPA with monthly intent (or vice versa) makes the months figure off by ~12x.
Dividing by per-subscription price rather than per-account ARPA misstates recovery for multi-product accounts.
Ignoring NULLIF on a zero/near-zero margin yields infinite or absurd payback — flag as 'undefined' instead.
metrics.sane_range: months, positive; healthy SaaS payback is <12 months, with <18 acceptable for enterprise; >24 signals weak unit economics or a grain/period error.

metrics.name: Activation / Trial-to-Paid Conversion Rate
metrics.definition: Share of trials or free signups (in a starting cohort) that convert to a paid subscription within the measurement window.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT converted_account_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT trial_or_signup_account_id), 0), where 'converted' iff the account started a paid subscription, measured on the SAME cohort of signups.
metrics.grain: Signup-cohort grain. Denominator = distinct accounts that started a trial/free plan in the cohort window; numerator = the subset that later converted to paid. A conversion rate is bounded 0..1 BY CONSTRUCTION.
anti_patterns: COUNT(paid) / COUNT(trials) as raw, unaligned totals → can exceed 1 (classic >1 bug); if you get >1 the cohort/join is wrong, not the data.
Counting paid SUBSCRIPTION rows instead of distinct converted accounts fans out the numerator (a converter who buys multiple products counts once).
Comparing this period's conversions to a different period's signups breaks the cohort and biases the rate.
Counting accounts that paid but never had a trial in the denominator population (population mismatch) skews the ratio.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (never > 1); opt-in free-trial B2B SaaS often 0.15–0.25, freemium B2C typically low single-digit percent.
needs_concepts: trials / free signups with start date
